worth_it_explanation: Performance Max spans every Google channel from one campaign and leans heavily on automation, so a few setup controls decide whether it complements the account or competes with it. Missing brand exclusions let it claim cheap brand conversions and flatter its own numbers, missing account-level negatives let it serve on queries the business does not want, and thin asset groups limit what it can build. The check reads those controls. The fixes are settings and asset changes rather than a rebuild, and they steer a campaign that otherwise optimizes in the dark.
intro: Performance Max runs across Search, Shopping, Display, YouTube, Gmail, Maps, and Discover from a single campaign, with Google's automation choosing where and to whom it shows. That reach makes a handful of guardrails important: brand exclusions keep it from spending on the business's own name, account-level negative keywords keep it off unwanted queries, and audience signals plus strong assets give the automation good material to work with. These checks confirm those controls are in place so the campaign complements the rest of the account rather than competing with it.

audit_checks.title: Performance Max has no brand exclusions, so it claims brand traffic
audit_checks.impact_rationale: Without brand exclusions, Performance Max can serve on the business's own brand searches, which convert cheaply and make the campaign's reported performance look stronger than the new demand it actually found. The brand conversions also pull budget toward easy wins rather than the prospecting Performance Max is meant to do.

educate.base:
Performance Max can serve on Search and Shopping queries, including searches for the business's own brand. Brand searches convert cheaply because the person already knows the company, so when Performance Max takes them, its cost per conversion looks low and its reported return looks high. That flatters the campaign without reflecting the new customers it found, and the cheap brand conversions pull the automation toward spending on brand rather than on the prospecting the campaign is meant to do.

Brand exclusions are the control that keeps Performance Max off the business's own brand terms. The account builds a brand list, then applies it to the campaign, and Google blocks the listed brand and its common misspellings and variants from Search and Shopping inventory. Without the exclusion, brand defense and prospecting blur together inside one campaign that cannot be told apart in reporting.

Brand exclusions and brand lists live in the campaign settings and the shared library. A few signs point to a missing exclusion:
  • The campaign has no brand list applied under its brand settings
  • The Performance Max search categories show the business's own brand among the top terms
  • The campaign's cost per conversion is far below the account's non-brand campaigns
Applying a brand exclusion list separates brand defense from prospecting so each can be measured and funded on its own. You may want to confirm whether a separate brand campaign already covers the brand searches before excluding them, so the brand demand is still captured somewhere deliberate.

site_type_overlays.ecommerce: On a store, Performance Max on the catalog can absorb shoppers searching the store's own brand, which inflates return on ad spend. You may want to apply brand exclusions so the catalog campaign is measured on the new demand it finds rather than on existing brand shoppers.
site_type_overlays.lead_gen: On a lead-generation account, brand searches are often the cheapest leads, so an unexcluded Performance Max campaign can look efficient while doing little prospecting. You may want to exclude brand and keep a dedicated brand campaign so the two goals stay separate.

learn_more.source_title: Apply brand exclusions to Performance Max or Search campaigns
About brand settings for Search and Performance Max

investigate.intro: Confirm whether a brand exclusion list is applied to the Performance Max campaign.

google_ads_ui.label: Google Ads UI
steps: Open the Performance Max campaign's settings and find the brand exclusions or brand settings section.
Note whether a brand list is applied or whether the field is empty.
Open the campaign's search categories or insights and look for the business's own brand among the top terms. Brand terms serving with no exclusion applied is the pattern to flag.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner records whether a brand exclusion list is applied to the Performance Max campaign and whether brand terms appear in its search categories. A campaign with brand excluded, or with brand deliberately handled elsewhere, passes. A campaign serving on brand with no exclusion is a fail recorded with the brand terms observed.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record whether a brand exclusion list is applied to the Performance Max campaign to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that {{site_name}}'s Performance Max campaign has no brand exclusions applied, so it can serve on the business's own brand searches. Those cheap brand conversions make the campaign look more efficient than the new demand it found and pull budget toward easy wins rather than prospecting. {{voice_recommend}} building a brand list and applying it as an exclusion, while confirming a dedicated brand campaign still captures the brand searches, so prospecting and brand defense can be measured and funded apart.

audit_checks.title: No account-level negative keywords apply to Performance Max
audit_checks.impact_rationale: Performance Max takes limited direct negative keyword input, so account-level negatives are the main way to keep it off unwanted queries. Without them, the campaign can spend on irrelevant or unsafe searches, and the account has little visibility into where that spend went.

educate.base:
Performance Max gives the practitioner less direct control over keywords than a Search campaign, so the levers that do exist matter more. Account-level negative keywords are one of the main ones. They apply across the account's Search and Shopping inventory, including Performance Max, and they are how a practitioner keeps the automated campaign off queries the business does not want.

When no account-level negatives are set, Performance Max can spend on searches that are irrelevant, competitor-focused, or unsafe for the brand, and its limited reporting makes that spend hard to spot. The campaign optimizes toward conversions, but it has no instruction about which queries to avoid, so it learns the boundaries only by spending against them.

Account-level negative keywords live in the account settings under negative keywords. A few signs point to a missing foundation:
  • The account-level negative keyword list is empty or very short
  • The Performance Max search categories show irrelevant or competitor queries
  • No standing list excludes job, free, or off-topic searches across the account
Building an account-level negative keyword list gives Performance Max the guardrail it cannot get through direct campaign negatives. You may want to start the list from the recurring off-target queries in the search terms and Performance Max search-category reports, so the negatives reflect real waste.

site_type_overlays.ecommerce: On a store, account-level negatives can keep Performance Max off searches for competitors and marketplaces that the catalog should not pay for. You may want to seed the list with competitor and marketplace names so the automated campaign stays on sellable demand.
site_type_overlays.lead_gen: On a lead-generation account, account-level negatives for job, salary, and do-it-yourself queries keep Performance Max from spending on people who will not become leads. You may want to apply those exclusions account-wide so they also cover the automated campaign.

learn_more.source_title: How to use brand suitability features in Performance Max

investigate.intro: Confirm whether account-level negative keywords are set so they apply to Performance Max.

google_ads_ui.label: Google Ads UI
steps: Open the account settings and find the account-level negative keywords section.
Note whether a meaningful list exists or whether it is empty.
Open the Performance Max campaign's search categories and look for irrelevant or competitor queries. An empty account-level list with off-target queries serving is the pattern to flag.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner records whether account-level negative keywords are set and whether Performance Max is serving on unwanted queries. A maintained account-level list that covers the obvious exclusions passes. An empty or thin list, with off-target queries appearing in the search categories, is a fail recorded with example queries.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record whether account-level negative keywords are set and whether Performance Max serves on unwanted queries to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that {{site_name}} has no meaningful account-level negative keywords, which are the main way to keep Performance Max off unwanted queries. The automated campaign can then spend on irrelevant, competitor, or unsafe searches with little visibility into where the money went. {{voice_recommend}} building an account-level negative keyword list seeded from the recurring off-target queries in the search-category reports, so Performance Max has a guardrail it cannot get from direct campaign negatives.

audit_checks.title: Asset groups are thin and carry no audience signals
audit_checks.impact_rationale: Performance Max builds ads from the assets and learns faster from audience signals, so a group with few headlines, images, or videos and no signals gives the automation little to work with. The campaign still runs, but it reaches fewer placements well and takes longer to find the right audience.

educate.base:
An asset group is the set of headlines, descriptions, images, logos, and videos that Performance Max assembles into ads across its channels. The automation mixes these to fit each placement, so the range of assets is the range of ads it can build. A thin asset group, with a handful of headlines and no video, limits the campaign to a narrow set of placements and weaker combinations.

Audience signals are the other input. They are not targeting; they are a hint that tells Performance Max which audiences, search terms, and data segments are likely to convert, so the automation starts learning from a warmer place rather than exploring blind. A group with strong assets but no audience signal still works, though it takes longer to find the right people.

The asset group view shows the asset counts and the Ad Strength, and the audience signal is set within each asset group. A few signs point to a group that gives the automation too little:
  • The asset group shows a low Ad Strength or a small number of headlines, images, or videos
  • No video asset is present, so Google auto-generates one from the other assets
  • The asset group has no audience signal attached
Filling out the assets and adding a relevant audience signal gives Performance Max more to build with and a faster start. You may want to supply at least one real video rather than relying on the auto-generated one, so the YouTube placements show a deliberate creative.

site_type_overlays.ecommerce: On a store, a Performance Max campaign with a product feed still benefits from strong text, image, and video assets for its non-Shopping placements. You may want to confirm the asset group is full and carries an audience signal built from past purchasers or cart abandoners.
site_type_overlays.lead_gen: On a lead-generation account, the audience signal can draw on a Customer Match list of past leads or converters to point the automation at similar people. You may want to attach that signal and round out the assets so the campaign learns faster.

investigate.intro: Review each Performance Max asset group for asset coverage, Ad Strength, and an audience signal.

google_ads_ui.label: Google Ads UI
steps: Open the Performance Max campaign and review each asset group's asset counts and Ad Strength.
Note any group with few headlines, images, or videos, or with no video asset of its own.
Check whether each asset group has an audience signal attached. A thin asset group with no audience signal is the pattern to flag.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner records, per asset group, the asset coverage, Ad Strength, and whether an audience signal is attached. A group with full assets and a relevant signal passes. A thin group, or one with no audience signal, is a fail recorded with the asset group and what it lacks.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record each asset group's asset coverage, Ad Strength, and audience signal to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that {{site_name}}'s Performance Max asset groups are thin and carry no audience signals, so the automation has little to build with and no hint about who converts. The campaign still runs, though it reaches fewer placements well and takes longer to find the right audience. {{voice_recommend}} filling out the headlines, images, and video in each asset group and attaching a relevant audience signal, such as past converters, so Performance Max has more to work with and a faster start.
